macro_segments.title: Three Sources of Shlichut and Why Terumah's Pasuk is Needed

micro_segments.title: Introduction: Three Biblical Sources of Shlichut (Terumah, Gerushin, Korban Pesach)
The Gemara's Question: Why Do We Need the Pasuk by Terumah if We Already Have Korban Pesach and Gerushin
Rabbi Yannai's Teaching: The Word 'Gam' (Even) Teaches That a Shaliach Must Be Like You (Jewish)
Can We Learn This From Logical Reasoning: Rabbi Chiya bar Abba in the Name of Rabbi Yochanan
The Problem: Non-Jews Can Validly Separate Terumah, So Why Exclude Them as Shaliachs
The Resolution: The Pasuk Teaches Both That a Shaliach Must Be Jewish AND Must Be In the Category of Law
Rabbi Shimon's Position: Non-Jewish Terumah Has No Status, So the Pasuk Would Be Unnecessary

macro_segments.title: Excluding Non-Agents From Terumah: Sharecroppers, Partners, and Overseers

micro_segments.title: The Word 'Atem' (You) Excludes Certain Categories: Sharecroppers, Partners, and Overseers Cannot Take Terumah for Others
The Question: Why Would We Think a Shaliach is Excluded When the Word 'Atem' Excludes These Other Categories
The Answer: The Word 'Gam' (Even) Teaches That a Shaliach Works Here Too

macro_segments.title: Why We Cannot Learn Shlichut From Korban Pesach When Rabbi Yonatan Uses That Pasuk for a Different Law

micro_segments.title: The Problem: Rabbi Yonatan Uses the Pesach Pasuk to Prove One Doesn't Need a Kezayis to Be Yotzei
If That's What the Pasuk Teaches, We Don't Have Shlichut for Kodashim (Sacred Things) From Pesach
The Scene Itself Describes Shlichut: One Person Shechts for Everyone, Even if That's Not the Pasuk's Main Point

macro_segments.title: Why We Cannot Learn Shlichut From Korban Pesach When Rabbi Yonatan Uses That Pasuk for a Different Law (continued)

micro_segments.title: Objection: But Everyone Is a Co-Owner of the Pesach, So One Person Representing Them Isn't a Proof of General Agency
Two Psukim About Pesach: One on Shechting, One on Purchasing—Neither Can Be About General Shlichut Alone

macro_segments.title: The Model of Shlichut in Communal Contexts: Corporate Identity vs. Individual Agency

micro_segments.title: The Case of Co-Owners: One Person Representing a Group That Shares Ownership Is Not Proof of General Shlichut
The Rav's Teaching on Shliach Tzibbur: Communal Prayer as Corporate Identity, Not Individual Agency
Difference Between Silent and Repeated Shemoneh Esrei: Individual vs. Communal Prayer

macro_segments.title: Learning Shlichut From Pesach Purchasing: The Pasuk Iyakchu Lahem Ish

micro_segments.title: The Pasuk 'Veyikchu Lahem Ish Se Labeit Avot': One Person Purchases on Behalf of the Group
Objection: Maybe This Too Is Based on Pre-Existing Co-Ownership or Connection
If Both Psukim Are About Cases With Pre-Existing Connection, Why Are Two Psukim Needed
The Extra Pasuk as Superfluous: If We Learn About Minors From 'Ish Lefi Ochlo', The Purchasing Pasuk Teaches General Shlichut
Rabbi Yitzchak: The Pasuk Teaches Only Men (Not Minors) Can Acquire Possession

macro_segments.title: Other Contexts Where Shlichut or Similar Principles Apply

micro_segments.title: Rav Gidel in the Name of Rav: A Nasi (Prince) Represents His Tribe in Land Division
Problem: The Nasi Represents Even Minors and Those Without Shlichut Capacity
Distinction: This Is Zchiyah (Acquisition), Not Shlichut; One Can Acquire on Behalf of Others Without Appointment

macro_segments.title: Court-Appointed Trustees and Orphans Dividing an Estate

micro_segments.title: When Orphans Divide Their Father's Estate, Beis Din Appoints a Guardian (Apotropus) to Represent Their Interests
Rav Nachman: Once Orphans Grow Up, They Cannot Protest the Division Made on Their Behalf
The Power of Beis Din: A Court-Appointed Trustee's Decisions Are Binding
Exception: If the Court Made a Significant Error in Assessment, the Division Can Be Voided
Distinction: Preference vs. Error—Missing an Acre Difference Is Not an Error That Voids the Division

macro_segments.title: Brothers Dividing an Estate vs. Court Division: Application of Ona'ah (Overcharge) Laws

micro_segments.title: When Brothers Divide Their Father's Estate, They Follow the Same Rules as Normal Purchase and Sale
Three Cases: Less Than a Sixth (Valid), More Than a Sixth (Void), Exactly a Sixth (Valid With Difference)
When a Shaliach Divides an Estate: If the Division Is Off, the Shaliach Can Be Told 'You Were My Agent Only for My Benefit'
Caveat: The Above Rules (About Voiding) Only Apply When They Did Not Agree to a 'Beis Din-Style' Division
By Land (Karka), There Is No Ona'ah (Overcharge); Exception for Error in Measurement, Not Price Assessment
Anything Measured, Weighed, or Counted Is Subject to Being Voided Even Below the Ona'ah Threshold if the Quantity Is Wrong

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Chiya bar Abba / Rabbi Yochanan
